pathophysiology_summary: Oral candidiasis is overgrowth of commensal Candida (usually C. albicans) when the oral flora/immune balance is disturbed — here after broad-spectrum antibiotics. Pseudomembranous thrush produces adherent, wipeable white curd-like plaques that leave an erythematous, friable base, with burning, dysgeusia and often angular cheilitis. It is benign and responds to topical antifungals. The ground-truth tasks are (1) recognising it and distinguishing it from leukoplakia (non-scrapable, fixed) and aphthous ulcers (discrete ulcers), (2) screening for oesophageal extension (deep retrosternal odynophagia / food sticking — which needs systemic antifungal and suggests significant immunosuppression), (3) managing dehydration from pain-limited intake (patient living alone), and (4) considering an underlying predisposing condition (uncontrolled diabetes, HIV, immunosuppression, inhaled steroids) if there is no clear cause or it is recurrent/severe.

differential.evidence_basis: Scrapable white plaques with a red base after antibiotics, with angular cheilitis.
Screen for deep odynophagia/food sticking — needs systemic antifungal and suggests immunosuppression.
Leukoplakia is fixed/non-scrapable — argues against here.
Discrete ulcers, not diffuse wipeable plaques — argues against.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Oral thrush leads; oesophageal extension must be screened and underlying immunosuppression considered.

differential.evidence_basis: Scrapable plaques/red base after antibiotics, no deep odynophagia, no obvious immunosuppression — post-antibiotic oral thrush; dehydration risk from pain.
No deep odynophagia/food sticking; escalation target if they develop.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative oesophageal screen, oral thrush leads; topical antifungal + analgesia-enabled hydration, with oesophageal/dehydration safety-nets and an immunosuppression consideration.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; clears with topical antifungal; investigate for predisposing conditions if recurrent/unexplained.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is post-antibiotic oral thrush.

red_flags.description: Oesophageal candidiasis — deep retrosternal odynophagia or dysphagia (food sticking), suggesting oesophageal extension ± significant immunosuppression
red_flags.clinical_significance: Needs systemic antifungal and evaluation for immunosuppression (e.g. HIV).

red_flags.description: Dehydration from pain-limited intake — unable to drink, reduced urine output, lightheadedness (living alone)
red_flags.clinical_significance: May need ED for analgesia/IV fluids.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not screening deep odynophagia; unexplained thrush should prompt consideration of diabetes/HIV/immunosuppression.
Focusing on the mouth and missing pain-limited fluid intake in a patient living alone.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Topical antifungal + analgesia-enabled hydration; ED for oesophageal features or failed rehydration; GP work-up if unexplained/recurrent (diabetes/HIV).

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Oral thrush: topical antifungal (nystatin suspension or miconazole gel) for 7–14 days; treat angular cheilitis; investigate predisposing factors if recurrent/unexplained. Oesophageal involvement requires systemic antifungal (e.g. fluconazole) and evaluation for immunosuppression.
